metadata.title: Why the Drug Reached the Tumor and the Tumor Still Grew Back
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine candidate 06. Source: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/02-tumor-transport-barriers.md (outward interstitial fluid pressure mechanism and inside-out regrowth only).
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = drug / particles accumulating at the rim; CRIMSON = unreached core / outward pressure / regrowth; GOLD = the pressure gradient highlight
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: diffusion limit derivation (1-2mm threshold), four-barrier list, 30-vs-150nm size debate, and radiation resistance. Keep ONLY: the outward-pressure mechanism (leaky vessels + no lymphatics -> fluid buildup -> net outward flow -> particles pushed back to rim) and the inside-out regrowth payoff (hypoxic core survives, selection for resistant cells, tumor regrows from core).
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A nanoparticle drug reaches a mouse tumor. The vessels are leaky — particles accumulate. The outer rim clears beautifully. Weeks later, the tumor doubles in volume — growing from the inside out.
MRI shows a two-millimeter viable core at week three. The drug was there. The whole-organ measurement said it had accumulated. The core is untouched. Then the core grows back.

shot.image_description: An MRI slice of a mouse tumor rendered in editorial flat-print style on aged newsprint. The outer rim of the tumor is dark, indicating drug-killed tissue. A small, lighter circular core in the center remains viable. An annotation ring in crimson circles the core. Camera pushes in slowly toward the core.

props.headline: MRI shows a two-millimeter viable core at week three. The drug…

beats.narration_text: A drug reaches the tumor. Vessels are leaky and particles accumulate. The outer rim is cleared. Weeks later, the tumor regrows from the inside. The drug was there. Why did the core survive?

The naive picture: leaky vessels let particles in, and particles spread inward through the tissue. That is not wrong — it is just half the story. The same leaky vessels create a second effect no one celebrates.
Leaky vessel walls pour fluid into the tumor faster than the lymphatic system can drain it. Tumor lymphatics are broken or absent. The fluid has nowhere to go — so it stays, and pressure builds.

Fluid moves down pressure gradients. The tumor core accumulates fluid and reaches pressures five to ten times higher than normal tissue. Net flow is outward — from the pressurized core toward the tumor edge. A particle trying to move inward is swimming against that current.

So particles extravasate near the rim vessels, and the outward current carries them back. They pile up at the periphery. The core stays particle-free — not because the EPR effect failed, but because it succeeded and then the pressure it created pushed the particles away.

That unreached core is also hypoxic — starved of oxygen. Hypoxia selects for cells that tolerate stress, evade death signals, and survive. The cells the drug never reached are not a random sample. They are the tumor's most resistant survivors.
The rim cells the drug killed were not the cells most likely to cause recurrence. The core cells it never reached were — and the hypoxic pressure had spent months making them harder to kill.

Take an illustrative scenario. A nanoparticle therapy shrinks a mouse tumor rim by sixty percent over three weeks. At week three, IFP at the core measures twenty-five millimeters of mercury. At the rim: five. The core — viable, two millimeters across — sits behind a pressure wall the particles cannot cross. By week eight, tumor volume has doubled. The growth originates from the core.

The drug reached the tumor. The leaky vessels that let it in also flooded the interstitium and drove fluid outward. Particles stayed at the rim. The core survived, selected for resistance, and grew back. The accumulation was real. The delivery was not.

beats.narration_text: Part of the Cancer Nanomedicine series.
